Hey, this is Sean and Andre. This is the Momentum Builder Workshop Week 3 landing page review. Today we have chosen one landing page to review just because last time we did four Facebook ads and it was over two hours. So we're trying to do just 30 minutes today. We have one lined up. If we do that in under 30 minutes, we might add a second one. What we really want to do is make the review broadly useful for everyone. We've chosen a landing page that was submitted by Yvonne. So all of you who see the comments, you'll be able to click through and see the landing page itself. You listen to this. Definitely recommend that when you're listening to this audio, you open up a copy of the landing page as well. And Yvonne, if you're listening to this, don't make any edits to that original. That one, create a new version for the edit so that this makes sense as we go through. So that would be kind of funny if she changed the entire thing and nothing we're saying makes any sense by the time the recording was live. So all right, I'll start. I'll give a broad overview of the topic. What I'll do is I think I'll read the headline in two paragraphs just to orient things and then I'll hand it off to you, Andre, for your thoughts to get started. And we'll just go back and forth and see where things go. All right. So here's the headline. People pick an idea and start a business as flawed. Maybe they begin at the wrong place and in the wrong way. Perhaps there's a better way. Spiring entrepreneurs often fail because they don't start with the foundation. They ignore important elements. They don't find the right idea that is in sync with their interests or unique strengths. They don't define a niche of people where they can solve their problems. They don't know if their product or target market has a willing audience who wants what they have and will pay for it. And importantly, they don't have a success mindset. All right. So those are the first few paragraphs. This is, I don't know exactly how many words it was. I think it was like five pages or so, four and a half pages. Yeah, four and a half pages. 1200 words. Okay. And there are lots of ingredients here, but, but if that's the thesis, you need to make your case. You're probably starting at some high level. That's the call to action for the opt-in. And once you have that, then you just write to those points and make the case for those points in that order because you know the order is right. You know that it's step one, step two, step three, or point one, point two, point three, that there's a logical, there's good landing pages need logic and emotion. The logic often is the structure. It's the thing that can grow and it's the thing that makes the points hang together. And then the emotion is what we write around the logic. It's the texture, it's the stories, it's the things that add life to it, but it can't just be life and it can't just be logic. It's those two things together. I'm finding myself confused. I want it to be sequential. I want to feel like I'm being pulled forward. And in order to do that, in order to feel that, the writing has to represent that at its core structural level. And when it doesn't, we sense that. We've all read something like that where we start reading it and then we just kind of drift away because we're not being pulled forward. And a lot of that is because we're going back and forth a little bit. I had that from looking for the place where that happened. I'm thinking to myself, all right, tell me more. And then you tell me more. They ignore important elements on and on and on. But then 10 paragraphs later, it's the same thing again. So now I'm confused. Now, and even if it's not overt confusion, now I feel like, well, wait a second. Right. Like, that's where we started. So how do, how do we end where we started? You can't make progress. It becomes like an MC Escher painting where like we're going up a set of stairs, but we end up at the base of the stairs, right? So that we just did a little MC Escher thing in the writing where you took me on a journey, but where we, where we ended up was exactly where we started with the same point. So that's, that's how structure goes sideways a little bit. Yeah. Yeah. All right. I'm handing it off to you for the next thought. Okay. So I'm going to just restate a few things that we both said already. So in my mind, there are three things that are lacking with this page, with this landing page and the first in no order, maybe, although maybe that maybe it is an order. So first is structure so that logical or order of arguments, because without structure, it's just all over the show. And with structure, you know, then there will be more congruence and somebody can move through it and it will pull them forward, even if, even if the actual elements themselves need work, at least they're in an order that, that, that each, each, each next one supports the previous one and the overall message and pull someone forward. The second thing, which I really mentioned is the targeting. I think the, you know, who you're talking to informs your entire message, your thesis, everything. And it just feels like this, this, this message isn't talking to anyone specific. It's talking to many broad people and therefore it can't be specific because if it was, a lot of this wouldn't even be on the page. And then three, which is really like the last of these, the, not the least important, but it's the thing that you can, you can layer on last, which is the emotional, you know, piece. So the, the narrative, the, the actual story you can inject into your afterwards. At the end, just to give it some, as Sean said, some, some, some texture and color, but yeah, structure, targeting. I think targeting for me is, is a big one because there's certain things that are set on the page that seem incongruent. The one that, that are highlighted, I've made a note on because it felt a bit jarring is other than the, you know, who it's addressed to is she stated that starting a business is not hard. It just takes work. And I thought to myself, well, it's not hard. It's not necessarily hot. That could be true for somebody who's been doing this for a decade or more, but it's definitely not true for someone that's starting out. And it seems like this is targeting people that are starting out. So, you know, for me, I would acknowledge that it, it is hard. This is, this is hard, but with, with the right understanding and information, you can, you can stack the deck in your favor. It's still going to be hard and it's still going to require a lot of work and just acknowledge that. I think that will pull people forward. So that, that felt a bit, there, there was some dissonance in, in saying it's not hard. I mean, you go to any of the, of the landing pages that I've got the big promises and one of their, one of their core themes or tenants on those pages is this is easy. You just need to know which buttons to press and that's, I don't think you want to associate yourself with, with, with those pages. Yeah. I'm going to throw an idea out that I use a lot. I don't think I've ever told another living soul this. I'm almost hesitant to say it, but whatever. It's a secret. It's a secret. It's a top secret. It's not, it's not real. I don't know. It's probably so obvious that now with all the buildup, I don't really like that's the stupidest thing I've ever heard. But I think, I cut, I, the way I refer to this to myself is I think in what I call the rule of threes. And for me, the rule of threes is it's just shorthand from that. I want to, I want to make sure that when I'm explaining something, I can do it in, in sort of in threes. Right. And I think this, I imagine this came from the three act structure of a, of a play and sort of that idea of, I think that's where it came from. But it's a, it's really, it's, it's helpful for me to sit down and I'll explain the rule of threes and then talk about some nuances quickly. So the rule of threes for me is that if, but I'm thinking about writing something or communicating something, I want there to be no more than three main points. Yeah. So there, if there's a controlling idea, it's just for the sake of argument that I need to sit down and rewrite this entire landing page with a material that, that Yvonne has already put on the page. So I would, I would approach this sort of like unconsciously, probably from the perspective of the rule of threes. So I would establish my thesis. So I might, I would probably do something around the idea of the way, the way people take an idea, I'm sorry, the way people start a business is flawed. I think that's too broad. But the way people start a, and I would have a particular type of business. So let's just say for the sake of argument, started online business, the way, the way a certain audience starts online businesses is flawed. That's my thesis. So then it becomes the rule of threes. I want to communicate three main points with that. So it could be something like, that's my thesis. And it's, you know, point number one is aspiring entrepreneurs often, you know, they don't start with a foundation. And then, you know, then point number two is I'm like scanning the copy here. It's the, the idea is critically important. And, and then the third point is I mindset and beliefs are critical. Something like those are my three points. I don't want to do more than that. And, but within each of those three points, I want three supporting paragraphs or three supporting pieces of evidence. So when I say aspiring entrepreneurs don't, you know, often don't start with a foundation. I want to have three points that, that communicate that. And then when I go to the next thing about the importance of the idea, I want three points that, that communicate that. And then, and if I, if, and if I need to write longer, like if this is going to be, you know, thousands and thousands of words, or if it's a very long sequence, then I might, within the three sub points, I might add three more sub points. Now, I realize that sounds incredibly formulaic. However, yeah, one of my, I really liked the quote from Picasso, you know, it's, I think it's some very exciting, learn the rules like a pro so you can break them like an artist, something like that. Right. So this is not a capital R rule that I follow. It's not like it always has to be three. You know, sometimes it's five. You know, sometimes the, the, sometimes the content has its own needs and that's fine. But the rule of threes for me is this foundational concept that it allows me to, to think about like, okay, here's the thing that I'm writing to. Here's the thesis statement. Here's the controlling idea, whatever it is. From there, I have three opportunities, three high level opportunities. One, two, three. So if I just grab you on the street and I have 30 seconds to make my point, I can tell you the controlling idea and then I can tell you the three main points and then I can give you a call to action. I can, here's what you do next. That's it. Like that's whatever. But if I have more time, I can then, I can tell you the first point, I can explain it. Then I can move on to the second point and I can explain it. And then the third point and I can explain it and then the call to action. And that's usually, that's, that's, that's a formula for like a thousand fifteen hundred words. And it sounds incredibly formulaic, but it's just, it's just a scaffolding. Right. You're still writing on the texture around it, all of the, like all of the nuance and the color and all, like all of the stuff is around there. But what that gives me is it gives me kind of this, this scaffolding. It just tells me, like, and it helps me do, it helps me understand progress. And structure and motion, forward motion. Because again, we, that, if I were to outline this and find out that in the first sentence, I talk about starting with the foundation and then the tenth paragraph, I, I, I talk about the foundation, the groundwork again. I would see that in my outline to be like, no, no, no, no, I, that's that point. Number one was foundation. That, you know, I went three or four or whatever points underneath that I've covered foundation. And then from foundation goes to the next thing. So it would give me the roadmap of how my ideas flow together. And often that, that roadmap, this is where it gets a little weird too, is often the roadmap is emergent. It's not deliberate. And I, and I think for Yvonne in this example, I think Yvonne had to write this, this, the way she wrote it so that she can now come back to it and structure it. That, that happens a lot. I write that way a lot. There's sometimes when I just don't know where I'm going and the writing reveals itself. And then the editing is when the structure comes together and other, that's an emergent, that's sort of an emergent way of writing. And that's, that's really the basis of the idea, you know, and Lamont's idea of the shitty first draft and like, oh, just get it out of your head and get it out of the page and then edit it later. That, that works. Other times for me, it's very deliberate. I sit down and I know, here's my controlling idea. And in order for that to be true, the following three things need to be true. And, and I can show that those three things are true or important or whatever with these three individual, individual elements for each. And that can seem very, that writing just to that outline can seem kind of cold. So that's where we add the nuance to it. And we, we wrap it with story and, and we, we put texture in there. So it doesn't seem so cold, but it is the structure. It's the skeleton. It's the thing that allows the piece of writing to hang together really well. I don't know if that was. Yeah. I love that. I have to, I asked if it was underwhelming and your first response was, yeah, it was. Like, wow, all right, thanks, man. No, man, I think that, that is so good. We need to include it. And in fact, I can't believe you, you didn't share that little secret when you were writing the, the, that lesson definitely needs to be, it definitely needs to be added. I don't know that I was thinking about it. It's not conscious. It's, I don't think it's conscious. It's weird. I like that. I like that a lot because it, it, as you said, it, it, it gives like a framework, like some, some scaffolding in which you can then insert points, arguments, wrapped up in a story or some, some emotional narrative points, maybe as, as the last layer. So, and, you know, once you've done that, and then you read a piece, a piece that's been, been through numerous iterations, there's no way to tell what the underlying structure looks like. You know, the underlying framework will be completely invisible. It, it won't, it won't show itself on the page because it'll be so well written because, you know, but again, let's not forget that this critique that Sean and I are doing, we are doing it essentially on a, on a shitty first draft. It's, it's not meant to be perfect. So our comments, even though they sound critical, you know, when Yvonne writes, version two and version three, it's going to be, you know, way better than this. It's, it's, you know, and it's a good point that, you know, both Sean and I write, you know, in fact, our, our first written versions don't even, we, we, we don't even share between each other. We, we like write them in private on a piece of paper and just to get our heads right and to get our understanding in some sort of order on a page. And then it gets expressed, you know, in digital form. And then we, then we start sharing it. And that essentially becomes our, our shitty first draft, which at that point isn't, isn't a bad draft. But yeah, it's, it's weird. It's, it's our shitty third draft. It's our shitty third draft. I love that, that, that, that, because I quite, I'm, I'm going to steal that like an artist. Yeah, right. Exactly. And that's, this is such an important thing to mention in really for everybody to hear, but also Yvonne for you to hear. This is hard work. Right. And when you see, you know, when people have given us Andre and I feedback about, you know, about our writing, it, that always feels good. But I, but also it's what, what you see at the end, the thing that you read at the end is not the thing that we created first. So what you're putting on the screen here is what you created first. It should not be perfect. In fact, it really shouldn't be very good because if it's really good, it means, but you didn't have enough time to do it really well, to be honest. So if I don't, I wouldn't know how to explain it if this were really good, unless you're just a naturally gifted writer who knew your topic so well backwards and forwards, you can just sit down and write prose. Like that's, that's rare. Some people can do that. That's very rare. But now this is what's set, this next step is what separates okay performance, you know, okay landing page performance or underperformance with really that next level of this is where things start to change. And what separates those two is to have the wherewithal to sit down and open up a new document, take the work that you already had, that you already did and realize all the ingredients are in there. You might have to flesh them out a little bit. But the ingredients are there. You've done step one for your landing page now. Step one's the hardest step. Now step two is to sit down and to go through that and tighten it up and then start asking the hard questions. And those hard questions are who questions like who cares? Why do they care? Why? Why? Why? Why? So when you, for example, you write your controlling ID, you get your open, your new document, you put your controlling ID at the top. Then you say, you know, let's just assume it says the way people start businesses is flawed. Immediately you're going to start asking what people, or you're going to start asking questions. What people? Who are they? What's true for them? What are they looking for? What do they feel like right now? Like you're going to hammer yourself with those questions. What kinds of business? How are they flawed? Why are they flawed? What is it that makes them flawed? Are they flawed after they're created? Are they flawed in the beginning? Is there something that this type of person does that just blows it up before they even had a chance to start? Like you're going to hammer yourself with those questions. It's uncomfortable, way less comfortable if somebody else is doing it to you. But you hammer yourself with those questions and you answer them. You ask and you answer and you ask and you answer. And I know for me, the experience that I have is at some point I get pissed and I'm mad at myself, but I get irritated. It's this righteous indignation where, and I'll finally blur something out to myself. And then I step back and I'm like, son of a bitch, that's it. That's the thing that I've been trying to find. That's the weird work that we do. That's what this is all about. And then once you dial that in, you've got that, you've got to have that first idea and you've hammered it and hammered it. Like, just imagine steel on an anvil. And that's what makes steel strong is the hammer over and over. And when you've done that, you get that idea really strong. You look at it, you think that is it. That's the right audience. That's the right thing that is going to draw their attention. Then you go through your work and you find the three points. Because now the three points are a journey. And you go and you look for like, what are the three main things I've said in this writing? And then what you might find is there weren't three, there were five. Okay. You'll probably also notice that two of those five are very similar. So how could you express those the same way? And I think you did that here. I think you're talking about they're not starting with a foundation. And then you get into this proper groundwork thing. But then there's this, then you're getting into mindset. And there's a lot of things that are kind of related here. So what you need to do next with this piece is go get your outline, combine things that are like each other, figure out what they, what the, how you would express the two things that are similar. How would you express them as one, you may have to move up a level conceptually, you'll express them as one thing. And then figure that you have to figure out the outline. First, from what you've already written, reinventing the wheel, you're outline what you've already written, and then go back to that outline. Well, then I think then go back to that outline and sense does it flow? Does A lead to B lead to C? Do the points within each, like, do they make sense? Does, you know, if you say you need to start with a foundation, and then they talk about mindset, does mindset follow the foundation? Or do you realize that mindset is actually part of the foundation? Like, you've got to do that to get the flow. But, and then once the flow is there, then you come back with a sledge hammer again, and you hammer those points. So let's just for the sake of argument, say that you decide point number one is aspiring entrepreneurs often fail because they don't start with the foundation. And then you ask your you just hammer away at that. Why? Why don't they start with a foundation? What the hell is a foundation? What does it mean to be foundational in business? It doesn't apply to every type of business. Like, you just ask an idea this analog, you do with a pencil. Sometimes I'll do a while pace around my office. I've got whiteboards everywhere, and I'm kind of writing in different places, whatever it is. The method doesn't matter. What matters is the madness that goes with the method, which is to hammer yourself over and over to prove. Because if you can prove to yourself that you've made an airtight case, that if any question that a prospect might ask you, you've thought about already, you've asked yourself 35 different ways, and you've convinced yourself that this the way you've expressed an idea is solid, you believe in it because you have hit it from every possible angle. That comes through in the writing. The writing feels strong as a result of that. So you get the dual benefits, the triple benefit, I guess it's rule of three, is right? Triple benefits. You get the very strong controlling idea. You get a structure that flows logically from A to B to C that brings them to the call to action. And you also get this airtight case that you've made. The things that you're saying are supported. When you've said X, you've given evidence of X. That evidence could be stories where you've seen it happen, your experiences where you know that, where you've experienced it, where you've seen others experience, or that there's evidence, objective evidence, whatever it is, you've made the case. So that if you were in a court, by the time you get to the end and you wrap up your summation for the jury, the jury is nodding their head up and down, like that's where you got there. That's the feeling that you want. You don't want to feel like you've said something and you hope nobody asks about it. Like someone says, well, wait a minute, that doesn't make any sense. You don't want that feeling. You want to think, you want to wait, you almost want to be excited and anticipating that somebody might disagree with something that you've said because you've so thoroughly pressure tested it, that you're waiting for the opportunity. Like this is conceptually not really, but you're almost excited for someone to say, yeah, I don't think that's true. I don't think mindset really matters. And you're thinking to back your mind, trust me. And then you could show 500 different ways where mindset actually matters, for example, that's the feeling that you want from this. The ingredients are here. You just need to structure them in a way that flows, I think. Yeah, okay. So I'm going to restate a few things. The one of which I've avet to learn the hard way. So, you know, Sean mentioned, you mentioned this idea of a foundational business. So when I was, when I started working with my, with my writing coach, my writing coach leader, and we were working on nonfiction stuff, and I'm sharing stuff with her, she says, so what's pop? And I said, pop is a pocket of people. She says, well, what's a pocket of people? And I said, well, you know, it's a, it's a group of people with a shared worldview. And every single time she caught me out is because I hadn't defined what a certain thing, you know, idea, acronym, whatever it is. And I know we, we get caught up in speaking our, our, our secret language that only our people know. But, you know, you targeting people from Facebook, you don't know who they are other than some, some targeting and some, you know, in an ad, but we can't presume that they get to understand our language, what we mean, especially when, when things are vague, like foundational, you know, that's, that needs to be defined more. So I think that's, that's a good point to find everything that's, that's not crystal clear. What's what else? I've re, I've reordered my, my broadly useful list for everybody. So I'll retell that all quickly. So I think the most important thing is to remember when you write on your page is targeting, because the targeting informs, you know, your, your message. So everything on the page is in service of a group of people who you think you want to serve. So you already understand who you think these people are, but obviously you then using Facebook ads to, to try and target them. So that, so that story, that, that language, the messaging on the page is all in service of that, that group. Next is the thesis statement, your controlling idea, again, that informs everything else below it. So your logical structure of arguments, as Sean said, your, the rule of three, all of that is informed by your argument, your thesis statement, your controlling idea. So, and then after that, it's sometimes it's, I find it easier to, to add emotional elements, you know, story elements after those first three have been, you know, so the, so the main structure or framework has been put on the page. And then it's like, okay, so what stories can I tell that is going to highlight the structural elements, you know, these, these, these, these arguments. Sometimes we use book references, so we'll, you know, if, if we're trying to explain, explain us, explain a certain idea, maybe it's a complex idea. Sometimes it's easy to, to refer to other people who have either books on the subject or just by mentioning their name, and they are associated with this idea. It, it, it, it helps communicate that message without having to restate it all on the page and blow it up the word counts. So I think that can be added in nearer the end. And then finally, the call to action, we haven't actually addressed this on this page yet. One of the things I found kind of weird was near, near the end of the page. There's a kind of like, I don't know, like a heading, who finds this important? And then there's one, two, three, and then there's four, four short paragraphs that are kind of the ending. Yeah, who finds this important seems like it's, it's, it shouldn't be, it shouldn't be, it shouldn't be, it shouldn't be like it's, it shouldn't be there if, if everything above has done the job of pulling people forward, because at that point people, the only logical step is they thinking is like, how do I click through to whatever's next? It's, it's, they can't wait to get the next thing. I don't think you need to say who finds this important because that would be, you know, that's going to be evident on the page. You don't need to be explicit about that. In fact, that kind of like, yeah, it's just, that's just one of the little elements of dissonance I found when I was, when I was scanning over this. Yeah, otherwise that's it, from my perspective. Yeah, I'll, yeah, I'll add two, we did, we do need to address the call to action. So I was thinking two things that I wanted to contribute, but then I'll, now I'm, now I'm thinking again in rule three. So I'll add that to my list. This is, I'm going to include this, but this is, this is, there's a lot in this one thought. So the three, these are, I'll do the three, the rule of threes here. So I want to talk about editing, I want to talk about musical quality of the writing, and then I want to talk about the cause to action. So now I'm organizing my head around the rule of three, and it's easy to comprehend that like there's three things we're going to go through and it, it makes sense. Like those three things make sense in order, but let's, let's talk about editing. You know, I, I tend to, my background in writing, I was a professional journalist, and I went from being an academic writer and graduate school to being a journalist, and those two things are not at all the same. And there was gallons of red ink on my first writing as a journalist because journalism is a very sharp, very concise language, very verb focused, action focused way of writing. It's a very powerful way of writing too. And I often find there's, there's a great, there's a, you see, you see it as a meme, you see all kinds of things and what it, it's, it's, it's three words, and it says omit unnecessary words and then unnecessary has a strike through, through it. Like that, there's a lot communicated in that one sentence that there's so, we put so many extra words in our language when we write that they don't belong. And I'll give an example from this actual text to talk about how you can tighten up your writing and make it more powerful. Fewer words, if you have the same number of ideas and fewer words, the writing becomes more powerful. That's just, it's a, a fact of life. If you have a thousand words and you make three points, and you can make those three, those same three points in 500 words, they hit much harder in the 500 words. That's just, that's the, that's the nature of powerful writing. So here's a good example. These are three, three paragraphs, three sentences that set up as paragraphs toward the end. Yvonne writes, words carry vibrations and energy, which we respond to. There's a scientific reason for this. As the brilliant scientist, Nikolai Tesla said, if you want to find the secrets of the universe, think in terms of energy, frequency and vibration. Another world famous genius agrees, everything in life is vibration and dash Albert Einstein. You could probably say all of that in a sentence or two. Like, let's look at some of the words we can get that are in phrase, but things we can get rid of. Words carry vibrations and energy that we, which we respond to. That's already said below. Everything in life is vibration, according to Einstein. So implicitly, that includes words and, and you think about, and if, you know, Tesla, if you want to find the secrets of the universe, energy, frequency, vibration, like, so we don't need to say words carry vibrations, energy, which we respond to. Someone else has already said it. We don't need to say there's a scientific reason for it. When we have two scientists who say it, one of them was probably the most famous scientist in the world. I would, I would, you could make this entire, you could make this same point with a single quote. So all of those three paragraphs could go away. And you could simply say everything in life is vibration. Albert Einstein, right? You don't need to tell, they don't need to say that he's a world famous genius. We know he's a world famous genius. Everyone knows who Albert Einstein is. You don't have to say that words carry vibration and energy because Einstein said it. Everything in life is vibration, right? So you can, there's, there's, this is really the, the micro editing, right? You can get in, you can just remove so much extra stuff. And when you do that, the writing becomes really powerful. So let's go on to the leader, the musical element too. So that this is almost like, this is, this is, there's, these are related because when you make your writing tight like that, when, when you communicate a lot with fewer words, then you start picking up on the rhythm of language. So the second paragraph, there's a, I think this is, this is one of the better examples. Let's listen to the, and I can't stress enough to all of you, read your writing out loud or read it in your head, but hear your voice saying it in your head with inflection and with the right pacing, because you'll, you'll hear funny things or you'll hear where it's off. Okay. So here's the paragraph. I'll, I'll tee up the first line. Aspiring entrepreneurs often fail because they don't start with the foundation. They ignore important elements. They don't find the right idea that is in sync with their interests or unique strengths. They don't define a niche of people where they can solve their problems. They don't know if their product or target market has a willing audience who wants what they have and will pay for it. And importantly, they don't have a success mindset. You can feel the beats in that, the, not the writing beats, the, the notes, you can feel the beats in that writing. There's a difference in power between they ignore important elements. It's four words. There's a lot packed in that four words. Then the next sentence, they don't find the right idea that is in sync with their interests or unique strengths. That's a different feel. It's not as powerful. Now, if you, you could, and you may want to mix up the rhythm, but you could, there's, you could make this paragraph or you can make it a line of points. You can make it feel very different if they all have the same format. So if you said they ignore important elements, they don't find the right idea. They don't define a niche of people properly. They don't have a success mindset. Right? That, you're, that's a reinforcing that actually pulls people forward because once you've said, once you, once you've written, they don't, they, they ignore important elements. They don't find the right idea. Now you, your, the brain is anticipating like, oh, I, I see that's it's for, it's like this, a certain number of notes, but when it, so if you want to pull somebody forward, you, you make the structure, pull them forward, you make the, the lyrical, musical quality, pull them forward. And then if you want to interrupt that flow, you change the structure. So you could say, they ignore important elements. They don't find the right idea. They don't define a niche of people properly. They don't have a success mindset. And then you could introduce something like that breaks all of that. And most importantly, they don't know if their product or target market has a willing audience who will pay for it. Now you've changed the dynamic because I'm like, wait a second, you're, you're, you're leading me forward. One, two, three, one, two, three, one, two, three, what, and then all of a sudden there's this powerful, it makes that, that longer sentence stand out. Now there's contrast in the writing and what, what is in contrast we notice. So if we can, this, this is, there's a lot to explore here, but just think about the musical quality of your writing. Read the sentences out loud and just, just note, that was a pun that I didn't intend. Just make a mental note of what does it sound like when you say the sentences and would it benefit from the sentences having the same four or five words as like you make a point, you make a point, you make a point. And then in often like a format that I see, and I think I tend to do this, is it's make a point, make a point, make a point, then summarize it as a conclusion from those points. And the conclusion is longer and the conclusion is the thing you want somebody to remember. That's the thing that, you know, it's almost like you're building your, you know, it's, it's one plus two plus three equals, and then there's the statement. So this is hard stuff to explain. It's just, it's a, think about that in your writing. And then finally we'll just hit, we'll call the action. I think what's both Riff on call to action. You mentioned it, I'll mention one thing and then you wrap up, you know, call to action. I think the call to action is the logical conclusion of whatever has happened so far. So it's not, like, you know, it's not the what's the most powerful thing I can say. It's not do I need to, you know, say sign up versus whatever. It's, you have led somebody. So if you create an outline of your Facebook ad to your, if you, if you create an outline of your Facebook ad and your landing page and then you know where they're going to, what they're going next in the first two emails, there's something that you can say between the landing page and there's something you can say between the Facebook ad and the landing page and something that you can say between the landing page and the first emails that is a natural call to action. And that call to action is whatever they're going, whatever is going to happen next that they want. That's how I think about the call to action. So it's not about the mechanics. And somebody has mentioned this and feedback that I need to include in one of the modules that someone emailed me and said, well, wait a minute, you know, you said you should mention if it's by email and other things on a Facebook ad. Yes, I'm not, we're not trying to hide things, but we're also not, we want, we want the reader to feel like each transition makes sense. It's just like one paragraph or one chapter ends, another chapter begins. That when the chapter ends, you don't want to walk away from the book necessarily. It has come to a conclusion, but the conclusion leads to the next chapter. So each of these is another chapter. So the call to action, there's no formula for the call to action other than to say that by the time someone has gone through this landing page, there should be a significant desire that he or she has that you have created that is only fulfilled when they take the next step into the first part of the email series. And it is that desire that becomes the focus of the call to action. All right, now it's your turn to make sense of that and wrap up today's call. I think you've said it all, man. Yeah, I mean, it's, there needs to be a sense of the logical next step is to do something. And I think you should be explicit in telling them what to do next. So there's this pull, this you've channeled desire through the words that you've written on the page that's all leading to this logical conclusion at the end. And it's just like a feeling when someone gets to the bottom, they either all in or they just can't be bothered. They can't wait to find the exit button. But for all the other people, if it's written well, you really just need to tell them what to do next. And that's it. It doesn't need to be spelled out or at least how it's shown over here, who finds this important. The people that find this important are going to find this important. That doesn't need to be stated. But yeah, so I think pretty much what you've said. I also want to just reiterate two other things you mentioned. One of which is, few words is writing with fewer words is always more difficult than writing with more words. And quite a few of the landing pages we saw were upwards of 3,000 words long. And they probably could have been a thousand words and been far more far better for it. Sean and I have a friend, I don't know, I suppose it's worth mentioning this. We have a friend, Mappasimans, and he writes long. Most of his articles are 10,000 words in that region. And it's very well written. When you read it, it's very well written. It's there's everything's in the right order. It pulls you down. But he does between 20 and 30 drafts. It is brutal, which is why he can get away with so many words. Our battle to write in the neighborhood that is that long. It's very, very difficult to write, to keep things punchy and compact. So it can be, creating constraints could be a good thing, especially when you're doing your second draft. So not this first shitty first draft. This first shitty first draft is just meant to get stuff out of the head onto the page, at which point you've got all the material or most of the material required to mold this into something that's a lot better. And normally that will be even, you know, it'll be a lot less. So at that point, you can create a constraint and say, okay, I've got 2000 words here. My constraint is going to be 1200 words and less if possible. And it just forces you to be brutal about cutting out stuff. It's always difficult. I hate to do it with my own words, because, you know, sometimes sentences just just feel right. And you've got a few of them to choose from. And just nothing seems to be worth deleting. But you've got to be brutal, because as soon as I share stuff with with somebody that that does editing for me, like my writing coach, she has no problems deleting stuff. So yeah, and then the and then the last thing I'll add. And this is something that I do with almost all my emails, because when I'm reading in my head, it is very different to when I'm reading aloud. In fact, I battle to read aloud. I just it feels awkward. So on the Mac, there's a it's built into the Mac. I'm sure there's a there's a version on Windows where you can hit hit some some keys once you've enabled the feature and it'll read selected text to you. You know, it's slightly robotic, but it's but it's pretty good. And I just speed it up slightly. So my Mac version, it's it's it's probably reading at 1.5 times. And then it sounds normal to me. But I'll do that. So I'll highlight chunks of an email. I won't highlight an entire email, because there's no pause, it just starts to read. And so I'll do like a paragraph at a time, or sometimes a few paragraphs, and then it'll read it. And I'll just listen. I'll just watch, I'll just follow because it highlights each word as it's reading to you. And as soon as there's, there's like a break and like, you know, Sean, Sean mentioned that, you know, there's there needs to be this this musical feeling to to the writing. And when it's when a computer is reading it to you, you can you can you can tell that. And yeah, so that's that's one of the tricks that I do. It's not really a trick, it's just it's just a thing. But it's very helpful because in my head, there's no spelling mistakes and the writing flows. But when it's read to me, it's terrible. That's what I do. How to make your writing experience even more terrible. I'm not sure that's the the value we want to contribute to everybody today. All right, I think that's a good that's It's a speed of hour. So that seems like, yeah, it seems like, you know, we don't, we don't want to go, you know, another hour and say the same things over and over again. So it listen when you listen to this, the hope is that it's there's just a few things going on one that you're you're looking at Yvonne's work and thank you Yvonne for being willing to submit your work that you're looking at Yvonne's work and then hearing what we're saying about that and also about what we're saying that could apply to what you've written. But like those are the two things that are always happening. And there's not this is not formulaic. There are no rules here that are always true. It's just that you develop a sense the rules become guardrails. They keep you sort of in the right place to find the best opportunities for solutions, but they don't guarantee anything. And then once you once you understand those rules, you can see that they're they help they really help you pull your work forward. And at that point, it's just a matter of refine and refine and refine. And at some point, you move on to the next thing. So I would all of you should plan I think realistically plan on three drafts for a landing page to get started. And then infinite changes after that, when it makes contact with an audience, you'll start to understand how the audience is responding. So thanks as always to Andre. It's great. Always a pleasure to do these reviews. We hope you guys have a good one. Thanks, Iman. Appreciate it.
